REFERRAL TO ENT [1208418751]
a 23 year old female with of recurrent pharyngitis, including a significant episode four
years ago requiring 3 course Augementin and 1 dose of Rocephin IM. Was offered a referral to ENT but she
did not seek ENT consultation as symptoms resolved. One year ago, she observed a stable white "polyp on
her tonsil" with no pain or discomfort, only occasional foreign body sensation.
Reason for Referral: Evaluation and treatment of cyst like structure on left tonsil

Jenevieve or guardian(s), if you are reviewing this progress note and have questions about the meaning or
medical terms being used, please schedule an appointment or bring it up at your next follow-up

Interpreter Services Used?: No. Provider speaks same language as patient - English.
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with
Pharyngitis
Pt presents to clinic following upon pharyngitis she has been battling. Pt states she is still not
feeling well. Pt still having tonsil pain, discharge, she feels like she is swallowing glass, stiff neck
and random fevers. Pt seen at urgent care on Monday and was given penecillin
Subjective
History of Present Illness
A
e presents for evaluation of tonsillitis. She was previously referred to the ER, where a CT
scan showed no masses but indicated a possible tonsil stone. She was prescribed steroids to address
inflammation and pus. Her symptoms worsened on Sunday, with her tonsils swelling to the size of "golf balls"
and becoming covered in pus. Although the swelling has since subsided, she continues to experience a sore
throat. She has been on penicillin for four days, which has been less effective than previous antibiotics. She
still has white pus on her tonsils but has not experienced any rashes or fevers above 99°F. She reports neck
soreness, a headache behind her right eye, ear pain, and ringing in her ears. Advil has been effective in
managing her pain.
She has not engaged in oral or vaginal sex for the past four years. Both Monospot and strep tests have
returned negative results. She does not have a cough and attributes her fatigue to a lack of energy drinks.
at UC Davis and consumes energy drinks.
She is overdue for varicella, influenza, and Tdap vaccines. She is also due for a Pap smear. She is employed
No past medical history on file.
No past surgical history on file.

Progress Notes by Moises Aranda, PA-C att
COMPARISON: None
TECHNIQUE:
Helical post-contrast CT images of the neck were obtained from the skull base to the lung apices with sagittal
and coronal reformats.
Contrast: 100 CC Omnipaque 350 was administered intravenously.
Total exam Dose Length Product for a 425 mGy-cm
Total exam CT Dose Index 13 mGy
FINDINGS:
Nasopharynx: Unremarkable.
Oropharynx and hypopharynx: Mild swelling of the oropharyngeal tonsils. Mild associated hyperemia, more
notable on the left. Suspect trapped secretions in a left tonsillar crypt.
Larynx: Unremarkable.
Parotid glands: Unremarkable.
Submandibular glands: Unremarkable.
Thyroid gland: Unremarkable.
Lymph nodes: Mild hyperemic lymphadenopathy left neck.
Vascular structures: Visualized portions unremarkable.
Soft tissues including subcutaneous tissue: [Retropharyngeal space preserved.
Osseous structures: Degenerative changes of the TMJ with flattened mandibular condyles bilaterally. Reversal
of the normal lordosis of the cervical spine. No canal stenosis or significant neuroforamina narrowing of the
cervical spine.
Trachea and airway: Airway patent.
Lung apices: Visualized portion clear.
Other: Mild mucosal thickening of the paranasal sinuses. Mucous retention cyst versus polyp in the right
maxillary sinus. Mastoid air cells are clear. Partially empty sella.
IMPRESSION:
Mild swelling of the oropharyngeal tonsils. Mild associated hyperemia, more notable on the left.
Suspect trapped secretions in a left tonsillar crypt. Direct inspection recommended.
Mild hyperemic lymphadenopathy in the left neck.
Assessment and Plan
Assessment & Plan
1. Tonsillitis being treating empirically as strep despite negative POCT strep tests.
- Symptoms: tonsils with exudates, sore throat, headache, B/L ear pain.
- Some improvement, exudates persist.
- Continue 10-day penicillin course.
- Alternate Advil and Tylenol for pain.
- Throat swab for chlamydia/gonorrhea given history of oral sex in the past.
- Blood work for mononucleosis.
- Referred to ENT for possible tonsil stone/polyp like structure on left tonsil X 1 year.
2. Health maintenance.

claude_vision.referral_reason: Evaluation and treatment of a cyst-like structure / polyp-like structure on the left tonsil present for approximately 1 year, in the context of recurrent pharyngitis and a CT finding of suspected trapped secretions in a left tonsillar crypt.
relevant_clinical_findings: 23-year-old female with history of recurrent pharyngitis
Significant pharyngitis episode ~4 years ago requiring 3 courses of Augmentin and 1 dose of Rocephin IM
Approximately 1 year ago, patient noticed a stable white 'polyp on her tonsil' with no pain or discomfort — only occasional foreign body sensation
Current presentation: tonsil pain, tonsillar discharge, sensation of swallowing glass, stiff neck, random fevers
Tonsils swelled to 'golf ball' size during acute episode, swelling has since subsided
Penicillin prescribed for current episode (4 days), less effective than prior antibiotics
White pus on tonsils persisting; no rashes or fevers above 99°F at time of PCP visit
Neck soreness, headache behind right eye, ear pain, ringing in ears
Negative Monospot and strep POCT tests
Physical exam: Tonsillar exudate 1+ bilaterally; bilateral superficial cervical adenopathy present

CT Neck with contrast impression: Mild swelling of oropharyngeal tonsils, mild associated hyperemia more notable on the left, suspected trapped secretions in a left tonsillar crypt — direct inspection recommended; mild hyperemic lymphadenopathy in the left neck
No hearing test needed per scheduling note
claude_vision.imaging_summary: CT Neck with contrast was performed. Key findings: mild swelling of oropharyngeal tonsils with mild associated hyperemia more notable on the left; suspected trapped secretions in a left tonsillar crypt with direct inspection recommended; mild hyperemic lymphadenopathy in the left neck; unremarkable larynx, nasopharynx, parotid/submandibular/thyroid glands, and vasculature; mucous retention cyst vs. polyp in the right maxillary sinus noted incidentally; degenerative TMJ changes with reversal of normal cervical lordosis. Full CT report text is included within the faxed progress notes (pages 9-10 of the fax).
missing_information: Lab results not attached (mononucleosis blood work, STI/STD throat swab, hepatitis C/syphilis/HIV labs ordered but results not included)
No prior ENT consultation notes (patient was offered ENT referral ~4 years ago but declined)
Urgent care visit notes (patient was seen at urgent care prior to PCP visit — those records not included)
ER visit notes (patient was previously referred to the ER where CT was obtained — ER documentation not attached)
Insurance/Medicaid ID fields partially visible but obscured in fax; full eligibility documentation not confirmed
Referring provider NPI listed as 'No NPI Available' for Sacramento ENT in the referral form (though NPI 1104537968 is listed for Aranda, Moises PA-C as ordering provider)

provider_urgency_label.label: urgent
provider_urgency_label.source: Multiple locations: (1) Referral form header on fax page 2 states 'Urgent' in bold under the Referral section. (2) Priority field on the referral form (fax page 2) states 'Priority: Urgent [2]'. (3) Insurance authorization priority status (fax page 5) states 'Priority Status: 1 - STAT'. (4) Sutter Health referral scheduling note (page 1 of Sutter records) references 'RE: urg ref expo' with response 'Not urgent.' from Keaton, Evelyn Legaspi, NP. (5) Sutter referral information (page 2) lists 'Priority: Routine' in the referral information section — NOTE: this appears to reflect Sutter's own internal re-triage determination after receiving the referral, not the original referring provider's label.

evidence: CT neck finding: 'Suspect trapped secretions in a left tonsillar crypt. Direct inspection recommended.' — this is a suspected benign tonsillar crypt finding, not a confirmed or suspected malignancy.
Tonsillar exudate 1+ bilaterally with recurrent pharyngitis history — no description of rapid growth, ulceration, fixation, or features suspicious for malignancy.
No airway compromise documented: 'Pulmonary effort is normal. No accessory muscle usage or respiratory distress. No stridor.' Tonsil swelling described as having 'subsided.'
No peritonsillar abscess identified: CT shows no drainable collection; retropharyngeal space preserved; no description of uvular deviation or trismus.
No sudden hearing loss: ear pain and tinnitus present but characterized as associated symptoms of pharyngitis/adenopathy, not acute sensorineural hearing loss.
No foreign body in ear/nose/throat confirmed.
No infant feeding issue or tongue tie.
Sutter Health internal triage note from Keaton, Evelyn Legaspi, NP states: 'Not urgent.' — corroborating that clinical review by the receiving ENT system did not identify urgent criteria.
criteria_check.provider_label: Urgent / STAT (marked by referring provider CommuniCare+OLE); however, Sutter Health receiving triage re-classified as 'Not urgent' / 'Routine' upon clinical review
criteria_check.reasoning: Although the referring provider labeled this referral as Urgent/STAT, the clinical content does not match any of the defined ENT urgent criteria (no confirmed/suspected malignancy, no airway compromise, no peritonsillar abscess, no sudden hearing loss, no nasal fracture, no foreign body), and the receiving ENT system's own triage nurse independently confirmed 'Not urgent' — therefore this is appropriately classified as STANDARD QUEUE rather than SECONDARY APPROVAL NEEDED, as the discrepancy between provider urgency label and clinical content has already been adjudicated by the specialist's triage team and a completed visit on 3/2/26 is documented.

claude_vision.summary: This is a referral for a 23-year-old female being sent to Sacramento ENT (Hasday, Steven Joshua, MD) for evaluation of a persistent white polyp/cyst-like structure on her left tonsil present for approximately one year, occurring in the context of recurrent pharyngitis. CT neck with contrast showed suspected trapped secretions in a left tonsillar crypt with direct inspection recommended, and mild hyperemic cervical lymphadenopathy. The referral was originally marked Urgent/STAT by the referring provider (Moises Aranda, PA-C at CommuniCare+OLE), but the receiving ENT triage team re-classified it as routine/not urgent; an initial completed visit is already documented on 3/2/2026, with two subsequent appointments canceled (4/16/26 and 4/20/26), suggesting ongoing follow-up scheduling may be needed.
